speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Lambert, May 3, 1995. Mrs. Lambert, you were saying that you were not allowed to compete in the '36 Olympics.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was happening with you and your family then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was so upset the way it was being done. By the way, the other two girls who competed, whom I knew well, they were told that Bergmann is injured and cannot compete.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bergmann, meaning you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me, yes, that that's the-- that was the version that the-- the-- the girls got. And they believed it until much later. So I was very upset about the way-- you know, it was a very strenuous two years, let's put it that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: I never knew, when I went to those training camps, how will the girls accept me now? Maybe the anti-Semitic propaganda in Germany has caught up with them. Or maybe this time, they'll make me feel not wanted or whatever. And it-- it-- every night, I was thinking, what would happen to me if I do compete? And do I have to stand up on that podium and say heil Hitler like all the others? It was-- it was a terrible time for me. I mean, psychologically, it was very, very rough on me. So when the whole thing was over, in a way, I was very upset. In another way, I was enormously relieved. All of a sudden, I didn't have to worry anymore about these things. But I figured, I have to get away from everything. Everybody was talking to me. Why-- you know, how did this happen? How did that happen? And I-- I-- I went away-- I went to a-- a small place, in-- a spa. And I said, I don't want to talk to anybody. I want to be left alone. And I was there for two weeks all by myself. Didn't talk to anybody, was just thinking. What am I going to do? What am I going to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, the first thing I have to do is get out of Germany. I don't want to be here anymore. And there is no future for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I came home, I told my parents, I want to get out. And my father had a friend who had come to the United States as a young man and had become very, very rich. And he came to Laupheim quite a few times.

speaker: interviewee
text: And while I was this celebrity, you know, Olympic candidate and all this kind of stuff, he said to me, any time you want to come to the United States, just tell me and I will sponsor you. So when I came back from that spa, I wrote him a letter that I'm now ready to accept his offer, and so on and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he wrote a letter back. Well, time is terrible here. And we're just getting out of a Depression. And maybe you won't find a job. And maybe things will get better in Germany. And why don't you rethink your whole situation?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was just so mad. And my father was very angry. So my father wrote him a letter, not a very nice and friendly letter. And he finally did send me the affidavit. And I took it to the consulate. And the consulate-- the consul was extremely interested in that story. That-- that's the American consulate, of course. And I think that had something to do with it that I got my visa so very quickly. I got my visa, I think, in January. And I decided I was going to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, in America your name was known, and you weren't aware of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Right. So I got ready. A few more of the Nazi chicanery-- if you bought a pair of shoes for, let's say 30 dollars or marks or whatever, you had to pay 100% tax on it. If you bought a-- a camera for $200, you had to pay $200 tax. If-- whatever you bought, they taxed you 100%, just to make money and make life miserable. And when you packed, they sent some kind of an official. And you had to have a list ready with every item that you were going to put in that trunk. And they checked it off to make sure that everything was OK. And when my brother left, the official was very nasty and very-- you know, he made life difficult. When I left, it wasn't so bad. The official that came, we knew. And he even accepted a beer. And everything you know, went OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went separately, or you went together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My brother had come, I think, in January. And I-- I left in-- in May.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your younger brother remained.

speaker: interviewee
text: My younger brother remained with my parents. And my father was not allowed to leave, because he did a lot of business with England and the United States. And they wanted that foreign currency come in for the German economy. So they had taken his passport away. And he-- he had a-- a work permit for England, where he had gone every year to do business. And he had this work permit. And it had to be renewed. So he went to the authorities and said, I need to have my work permit renewed. And I need my passport for that. And they gave him the passport-- and-- passport-- And as soon as he had the passport, he said, I'm going to get out of here. And they packed a little suitcase, and each one just you know, the most necessary things. And they-- they took off. They went from one town to another so they couldn't be traced. They ended up in Cologne. And from there, they were able to get on a plane, even though he didn't have all the papers that were needed. And I still don't know how he managed it. He either bribed the-- the airline, or some fellow said, well, let this poor family get out of Germany, or how, I don't know. But they did get to England with nothing. Left the house, left everything they-- they owned behind, the factory, whatever there was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was shortly after you had left.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was quite a lot-- a lot later. My-- my father was-- I think it was in 1940. My father was still-- you know, the Crystal Night when all the men-- all the men from 18 to, I think, 60 or 65 were taken off and sent to concentration camp. My father was-- was among them. And he was in there four weeks. We did everything we could, you know, write to congressmen, write, wherever we-- we thought we could be helpful.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew that he had been interred?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we knew somehow. My father-- my mother wrote a letter that she-- that my father was ill and that she would move in with an aunt [? Bethel ?], or whatever her name was. So we knew that he was gone. And we tried to get him out. I mean, she couldn't write it, of course, you know, say he's in Dauchau. We only found that out afterwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after four weeks, he was released. And the only thing I found out from him was that when they got there, they were-- all their clothing was taken away from them, their underwear and everything. They put them in striped prisoners' cotton uniforms.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they had to stay. Now, Dachau is very high. And it gets very cold. And this was in November. They had to stand outside from 5 o'clock in the morning until 11 o'clock at night every day. And when my father came out, he weighed 80 pounds. So he signed himself into the Laupheim Hospital, which was run by Catholic nuns. And the next day, the nuns came to him and said that you-- that he had to leave, because the authorities had told them he has to leave because he's Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he-- I think he went to a cousin's house to recuperate. And he was OK. But I think that his sudden death at the age of 66, I think, had something to do with what he went through. I mean, not only the-- the concentration camp, but the whole process, what-- what happened to him with the factory, and fleeing, and all this kind of stuff. So I blame the Nazis for his death, too. What did he die of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Had a very sudden heart attack, just, you know, 15 minutes and he was gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: So backtracking to your story, you left by boat or by plane?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, nobody flew then. That was, you know, boat. And there were a lot of other people on the boat that I knew. And it was kind of a fun time, if you didn't think back of that whom you left back and all this. But it, you know, took about 10 days. And it was OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then you landed in this country when?

speaker: interviewee
text: May something, 1937. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] And my brother was there at the pier, of course. And there were some relatives there. Everybody who had time, who was maybe unemployed or independently rich, which didn't happen too often. Whenever somebody arrived, they would all come down to the boat and greet you, which was a very nice thing to do. And it was-- you know, I came-- I came-- let's see now. I think it was in the morning. And there were-- must've been about 15 people there. And we went through customs. We found one custom man who we knew of-- we knew of, that he was very lenient with the German-Jewish refugees. So we just opened up the suitcase. He said, OK. And we-- we-- we-- we went through.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when I went to New York in the taxi, I said, oh, my god. You know, I'm coming from a very small town, coming into this, nothing but glass and these huge buildings. And I said, oh, my god. Is this where I have to live? And it was very-- it was frightening.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I got to the-- my brother had rented me a room at Western Avenue. It was $4 a-- a week. And I got the shock of my life. The doorman was a very tall, heavy black man. And I had never seen a black person in all my life. And he scared me to death. But you know, he was a nice fellow. We got very friendly. We couldn't talk to each other much. I mean, I-- I did study English in school, but not having been the best student in the world, because sports was more important to me than-- than English and mathematics, I didn't pay that much attention to my studies. And-- but I learned quickly, though. And we were able to communicate with each other after a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when he told me about the discrimination against black, then they were Negroes, I was so shocked. I said, how can this be in this country of the free? How can they discriminate against the people of a different color? And it was hard for me to accept. And I found that there was more discrimination-- or not-- not, maybe not discrimination but separation between Jewish people and Gentile people in this country than it was ever in Germany before Hitler came in.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, I never heard of such a thing, that-- that a Jew couldn't join a golf club or any kind of-- the New York Athletic Club, they didn't take Jews. This to me was-- was unbelievable. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] So-- but you get used to everything. And we lived in Texas for awhile when my husband was in the army. And we got into terrible fights with people about it. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met my husband-- oh, this was another one of those German things, you know. There must've been a lot of propaganda, again, against coming to-- to have the-- the Americans come-- come to the Olympics. So they arranged to have the best Jewish athletes come to a place and give them-- you know, to see if they could find another athlete who could compete in the Olympics. I mean, they knew very well that there wasn't any. They might have been the best Jewish athletes, but there wasn't one-- there wasn't a one good enough to compete in the Olympics. And they knew it very well, but it was just a-- a deception to-- to fool the other countries, again. So that's where I met my husband. He was a-- a long jumper of minimal talent. [LAUGHTER] And all-- I mean, the others, they were just-- there was nobody there. I mean, I hate to say this. And it seems like I'm bragging, but I was really the only one that-- that had any kind of world-class possibilities. But it was a nice time. We were there for a week. And he had another girlfriend. I had another boyfriend. So we weren't really interested romantically.

speaker: interviewer
text: But later on, we-- he came to see me before I emigrated. He said, wouldn't it be nice if we would meet again? And he brought another friend along. And I had been to-- gone to Frankfurt to-- to visit my relatives.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he came. And we looked at each other. And we-- we fell in love. That was it. And he had to go to Switzerland for another year. He's a-- was a medical student at the time. And the Germans let him finish his studies, because his father had fought in the first World War for Germany. So they made some kind of an exception for children of war veterans. And they let him finish his studies, but they wouldn't let him take his state boards. Now, without his state boards, in the United States, he would have had to go through four years of medical school again. And since I only earned about $10 a week at the time, I didn't think it was feasible. So he went to Switzerland for a year to get his state boards. And I gave him the-- the affidavit, or I sponsored him, because his rich relatives refused. That was another thing, that people over here don't know what was involved to get-- to immigrate into this country. It was very hard, very tough. And to find somebody to sponsor you was almost impossible, because they would-- would have been responsible for you if you couldn't have found a job. So they would have had to support you financially. And people weren't that anxious to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I borrowed $2,000 from different sources. $10 here, $100 there, until I got $2,000. And that's the minimum that the consulate, the American consulate, asked for for an affidavit. And I made out the papers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they-- they-- they approved it. And that was it. And then I returned all the money again. So he's really here under-- under false pretenses. But he did all right, served in the Army. He couldn't wait to get into the Army, into the American Army. So when he went down to you know, to-- to enroll, they said they wouldn't take him as an officer, because he was not a citizen yet. But he could go in as a private. But that he didn't want to do, because he had a friend who was also a doctor. And he went in as a private. And instead of employing him somewhere in-- in the medical field, they made him shovel coal or some-- I mean, some-- some really stupid things.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my husband said, I think I can do more good to work in a hospital than to-- to do some meaningless job. And soon, I think the same day that he got his citizenship, he went down to Whitehall, and he got his commission as a first lieutenant. And I was still an enemy alien, even though he came a year after me. But the Army needed doctors, so I guess they processed him a little faster.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as an enemy alien, I was not allowed to travel. So any time my husband would have been transferred from one place to another, I would have had to make an application to travel with him. So he finally wrote a letter and said, this was a ridiculous situation, that this-- the wife of a-- a first lieutenant in the American army should be an enemy alien, and would they-- could they do something about it. And so they gave me my citizenship, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you yourself came here in 1937.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1938. My husband came in August of 1938. And due to the German whatever, you-- you weren't supposed to get married until your husband had a job, right? I mean, that was the-- that was the-- the basis. You cannot get married until your husband can support you. Well, my husband got a job as a painter to paint in the Hotel Biltmore. He knew some German man who offered him a job as a wall washer or a painter. And a wall washer would get $18 a week and my-- the painter would get $24 a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my husband said, I don't know either one of them, so I'll be a painter. And he went to the Hotel Biltmore. And he-- he was a painter up on the scaffold. They were painting the ceiling.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he wanted to learn English so he could pass his state boards here. And all the other painters were German and didn't speak a word of English. He couldn't learn any English there. And they were a lot of Nazis there, too. So he said, I don't want to be there anymore. So he quit. And I had a job, so I supported the two of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of work were you're doing here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was-- at that time, I was working in a doctor's office as a physiotherapist. And it was tight, but we managed.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came to the United States, did you do anything in sports?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes. I-- I joined the Park Central AA, which wasn't a big deal. But I competed in 1937 in the American championships in high jump and shot put. And I weighed about 112 pounds. And if you ever seen shot putters, they're like this, you know, really big. And I won. There was one woman from Tuskegee. She thought she had won because I-- I did high jump at the same time, and I  had to come back and do my shot put afterwards. And she was first. And I came, this nothing, skinny, 112 pound person. And I-- I threw the thing once. And I had won. And it-- it was hysterical, because they took pictures of us. I must've been half the size of this woman. And a funny thing happened. Friends of ours in South-- South Africa went to the movies one night, and in the news reel, there was I and this woman. They showed-- showed us together. And they-- they-- they thought it was just the funniest sight ever. And I have tried to find the picture, but I never could. And then the following year, I competed again. And I won again the-- in high jump. And the following year, I was just getting ready in the morning to-- yeah, it's the same morning. I-- I just packed my gear to go to another championship. And word came over the radio that the war had started in Europe. And I said, there are more important things in this world than high jumping. And I quit right then and there. I didn't go. And that was the end of my career. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So then you married.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you lived the life of an army bride.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We traveled around. We were all over the place. We were in Missouri. We were in Texas. We were in Texas again. We were in Santa Fe, New Mexico. And I was pregnant a couple of times and had some miscarriages. And finally, in Santa Fe-- this is a very peculiar story. In Santa Fe, I became pregnant again. And I went to this obstetrician. And what she did, I don't know. We drove home from Santa Fe in a car. I drove part of it. And nothing happened to me. I mean, I-- you know, nine months later, I-- I had a-- a healthy baby. And several years later, I opened-- somehow, I saw that in the daily news, they always had these gruesome crime stories. And I see this name of this doctor. I don't want to mention the name. Her first name was Dr. Nancy and C. And she had kidnapped a child in Santa Fe because she needed money. Somebody was blackmailing her, and she needed money. And this was my obstetrician. And they caught her right away, because she really was very inept at it. And they caught her right away. And her license was taken away. She was such a fabulous doctor. It was really such an-- an-- an awful thing to happen to her. But I was very grateful for her that I, nine months later, I had a-- a healthy baby. And she was responsible for it. But I felt sorry for her, that her license was taken away from her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then, what-- what did we do then? We came back to New York, I think in 1947, '46. '46. And 1947, my first son was born. 1951, my second son was born.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was praying for that son in the worst way. I didn't want a girl, because my mother and I had a terrible relationship all our lives. And I figured if that's the kind of relationship you have with a mother, I'd rather have a son, another son, than a daughter. And to the great regret of my husband, who loves girls of all ages, we had this second son. And I was very happy about it. And I'm still very happy about it, because they're two great kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to your family here? They settled in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean my mother and father?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother and father.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother and father, they-- they were able to-- to go to England, as I said before. And when the war broke out, they-- the English took them, all the-- like they took the Japanese here, and took all the Jewish refugees, and I suppose the Germans, too, if they had any there, and put them into-- into a camp. And they separated my mother and my father. And my-- my brother stayed with my mother. And they already had the visa to come over here. And still they interred them. And the thing that made me hate the English so much, when-- they were supposed to wait for their passage to go-- to come here, and it was very hard, because there was-- there were already U-boats around. And it-- it-- the traffic between England and Europe and-- and the United States was very limited.

speaker: interviewer
text: So finally, there was a boat ready for them in Liverpool. And when they took them to Liverpool under guard, my mother and father, you know, criminals. And when they arrived in Liverpool, the-- the ship wasn't ready yet. So they put my mother-- my father into jail, and my mother into jail.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my little brother, who was about 12 at the time, they put to-- to a Jewish family who spoke only Yiddish. And German Jews really don't speak Yiddish. And he could not communicate with them. And after all the-- the hardship that a child had to-- had to go through, to-- to do this to a family, I thought was just so unbelievably senseless and rotten and mean. And I have never forgiven the English for that. I mean, there was no reason to put them in the jail. They weren't criminals. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was just until they could--

speaker: interviewee
text: Two or three days, but that was long enough for my-- for my, you know, getting-- my dislike for the English even a lot more. I thought that was a terrible thing to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then they didn't leave by boat. They left by plane?

speaker: interviewee
text: They left by boat. Oh, yes, they left by boat. And they came over here. And we kind of doubled up. My brother and my sister-in-law, they just had a little baby. And somehow, we moved into a $4 room, my husband and I. And they took over-- we had moved, my brother and my sister-in-law and my husband and I, we had moved together to Queens at that time. And we shared the apartment. And so we moved out. And my parents moved in. It was very tight quarters and everything. But you had to manage, because times were very tough. And it worked out. And my father started a new business again and worked. And he--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he do here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he made-- he was in the [? hair ?] goods business again the same way, you know, trying to make a buck here and there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they-- they were well? They--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they were fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: They adjusted here?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they adjusted. I mean, my-- my-- my father spoke English anyway from his doing business in England so much. And my mother happened to be a very bright person. And she spoke English very well, wrote English very well. And they made it their business to-- to speak English only, as we did, too. When-- when-- when we came over, I mean, English wasn't such a-- German wasn't such a well-liked language in this country. So we made it our business to speak English only if we could. And we all learned.
